edges.evidence_quote: In July 2013, Ahrar al-Sham had 10,000 to 20,000 fighters, which at the time made it the second most powerful unit fighting against al-Assad, after the Free Syrian Army.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham was the second most powerful rebel group after the Free Syrian Army.

edges.context: Jaysh al-Ahrar split from Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: Ahrar al-Sham cooperates with the Free Syrian Army; however, it does not maintain ties with the Syrian National Council.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham cooperates with the Free Syrian Army.

edges.evidence_quote: Ahrar al-Sham was the most prominent of these, and a member of Ahrar al-Sham's, Abu 'Abd Al-Rahman Al-Suri (aka Abdulrahman Al Soory), served as the Front's spokesman.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham was a prominent member of the Syrian Islamic Front.

edges.evidence_quote: It was the principal organization operating under the umbrella of the Syrian Islamic Front and was a major component of the Islamic Front.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham was a major component of the Islamic Front.

edges.evidence_quote: Ahrar al-Sham and Jaysh al-Islam were the main rebel groups supported by Turkey.
edges.context: Both were main rebel groups supported by Turkey, but no direct relationship between them is stated.

edges.evidence_quote: On 18 February 2018, Ahrar al-Sham merged with the Nour al-Din al-Zenki Movement to form the Syrian Liberation Front.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham merged with Nour al-Din al-Zenki Movement to form Syrian Liberation Front.

edges.evidence_quote: On 18 February 2018, Ahrar al-Sham merged with the Nour al-Din al-Zenki Movement to form the Syrian Liberation Front.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham merged to become part of Syrian Liberation Front.

edges.evidence_quote: While both are major rebel groups, Ahrar al-Sham is not to be confused with Tahrir al-Sham, its main rival and former ally.
edges.context: Tahrir al-Sham is the main rival of Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: Before 2016, Ahrar al-Sham allied with the al-Nusra Front, a now-defunct affiliate of al-Qaeda.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham was allied with al-Nusra Front before 2016.

edges.target: Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant
edges.evidence_quote: In September 2013, members of ISIL killed the Ahrar al-Sham commander Abu Obeida Al-Binnishi, after he had intervened to protect a Malaysian Islamic charity; ISIL had mistaken its Malaysian flag for that of the United States.
edges.context: ISIL killed an Ahrar al-Sham commander, indicating conflict.

edges.evidence_quote: In May 2013, Ahrar al-Sham alongside Al-Nusra, ISIL, and the Tawhid Brigade fought the Ghuraba al-Sham Front because of looting and corruption on behalf of Ghuraba al-Sham as well as disputes Ghuraba al-Sham had with the Aleppo Sharia Court.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham fought against Ghuraba al-Sham Front.

edges.evidence_quote: In May 2013, Ahrar al-Sham alongside Al-Nusra, ISIL, and the Tawhid Brigade fought the Ghuraba al-Sham Front because of looting and corruption on behalf of Ghuraba al-Sham as well as disputes Ghuraba al-Sham had with the Aleppo Sharia Court.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham fought alongside Tawhid Brigade against Ghuraba al-Sham Front.

edges.evidence_quote: In March 2015, the Suqour al-Sham Brigade merged with Ahrar al-Sham, but left in September 2016.
edges.context: Suqour al-Sham Brigade merged with Ahrar al-Sham in 2015, then left in 2016.

edges.evidence_quote: Later in September, Suqour al-Sham joined the Army of Conquest, a group which also has Ahrar al-Sham as a member.
edges.context: Both Ahrar al-Sham and Suqour al-Sham are members of Army of Conquest.

edges.evidence_quote: In early November 2014, representatives from Ahrar al-Sham reportedly attended a meeting with al-Nusra Front, the Khorasan Group, the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant, and Jund al-Aqsa, which sought to unite the groups against the Syrian government.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham attended a meeting with Khorasan Group to unite against Syrian government.

edges.evidence_quote: In early November 2014, representatives from Ahrar al-Sham reportedly attended a meeting with al-Nusra Front, the Khorasan Group, the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant, and Jund al-Aqsa, which sought to unite the groups against the Syrian government.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham attended a meeting with Jund al-Aqsa to unite against Syrian government.

edges.evidence_quote: On 26 April 2015, Ahrar al-Sham, along with other major Aleppo based groups, established the Fatah Halab joint operations room.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham co-established Fatah Halab joint operations room.

edges.evidence_quote: In October 2015, Abu Amara Brigades left the Levant Front, which they had joined in February 2015, and joined Ahrar al-Sham.
edges.context: Abu Amara Brigades joined Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: In October 2015, Abu Amara Brigades left the Levant Front, which they had joined in February 2015, and joined Ahrar al-Sham.
edges.context: Abu Amara Brigades left Levant Front to join Ahrar al-Sham; no direct relationship between Ahrar and Levant Front.

edges.evidence_quote: On 21 October 2015, the Jund al Malahim operations room was created as an alliance of Ajnad al Sham, Ahrar al-Sham and Al-Nusra in Rif Dimashq.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham allied with Ajnad al Sham in Jund al Malahim operations room.

edges.evidence_quote: On 21 October 2015, the Jund al Malahim operations room was created as an alliance of Ajnad al Sham, Ahrar al-Sham and Al-Nusra in Rif Dimashq.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham was a founding member of Jund al Malahim operations room.

edges.evidence_quote: On the following day, Jaysh al-Sunna's branch in Hama merged with Ahrar al-Sham, though its northern Aleppo branch was not a part of this merger.
edges.context: Jaysh al-Sunna's Hama branch merged with Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: In September 2016, Ashida'a Mujahideen Brigade left Ahrar al-Sham, apparently due to Ahrar's support of Turkey's Operation Euphrates Shield and lack of willingness to be closer to al-Nusra Front.
edges.context: Ashida'a Mujahideen Brigade left Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: On 10 December 2016, 16 Ahrar al-Sham units under Hashim al-Sheikh, known by the alias Abu Jaber, formed a quasi-independent group within Ahrar called Jaysh al-Ahrar, or the Free Army, for similar reasons as Ashida'a Mujahideen Brigade leaving 3 months prior.
edges.context: Jaysh al-Ahrar formed as a quasi-independent group within Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: On 23 January 2017, the Jabhat Fatḥ al-Sham attacked Jabhat Ahl al-Sham bases in Atarib and other towns in western Aleppo. All the bases were captured and by 24 January, the group was defeated and joined Ahrar al-Sham.
edges.context: Jabhat Ahl al-Sham was defeated and joined Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: On 25 January 2017, several factions from Jaysh al-Islam based in Aleppo left to join Ahrar, establishing the Ansar Regiment. On the same day, the remaining Fastaqim Union members of its Aleppo branch joined Ahrar al-Sham.
edges.context: Fastaqim Union's Aleppo branch joined Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: On 25 January 2017, Suqour al-Sham Brigade along with the Idlib branch of Jaysh al-Islam and the Aleppo branch of the Levant Front joined Ahrar al-Sham. On the following day, al-Miiqdad Brigade also joined Ahrar.
edges.context: Al-Miiqdad Brigade joined Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: On 6 August 2017, 120 Ahrar al-Sham fighters in Arbin, Eastern Ghouta defected to the al-Rahman Legion after internal disputes. Ahrar al-Sham accused the Rahman Legion of seizing their weapons, while the Rahman Legion accused Ahrar al-Sham of their attempt to implement their 'failed' experience from northern Syria in eastern Ghouta. A ceasefire agreement between the Rahman Legion and Ahrar al-Sham was implemented on 9 August.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham had conflict with al-Rahman Legion leading to defections, then a ceasefire.

edges.evidence_quote: On 22 June 2018, an Ahrar al-Sham commander was assassinated in al-Bab by gunmen believed to be part of the Hamza Division.
edges.context: Hamza Division assassinated an Ahrar al-Sham commander.

edges.evidence_quote: Ahrar al-Sham participated in the Operation Deterrence of Aggression which began on November 27, 2024, as part of the National Front for Liberation.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham participated in operations as part of National Front for Liberation.

edges.evidence_quote: On the 29th of January 2025, during the Conference for Announcing the Victory of the Syrian Revolution, Ahrar al-Sham declared its dissolution and merged into the Syrian Armed Forces.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham dissolved and merged into Syrian Armed Forces.

edges.target: Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant
edges.evidence_quote: Ahrar al-Sham had worked with ISIL until the two groups began their present-day hostilities with one another in February 2014.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham cooperated with ISIL until they became hostile in February 2014.

edges.target: Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant
edges.evidence_quote: Ahrar al-Sham had worked with ISIL until the two groups began their present-day hostilities with one another in February 2014.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham and ISIL became hostile in February 2014.

edges.evidence_quote: Before 2016, Ahrar al-Sham allied with the al-Nusra Front, a now-defunct affiliate of al-Qaeda.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham was allied with al-Nusra Front before 2016.

edges.evidence_quote: since late 2016, Al-Nusra and Ahrar al-Sham have been increasingly rivalrous, with military clashes between them taking place in the Idlib Governorate in January–March 2017 and July 2017.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham and al-Nusra Front became rivalrous with clashes in 2017.

edges.evidence_quote: From 2017 onward, it increasingly fought against Tahrir al-Sham
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham fought against Tahrir al-Sham from 2017 onward.

edges.evidence_quote: the group alongside the al-Nusra Front and Jaysh al-Islam co-signed a request asking the Islamic State's leader Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi to mediate a dispute over an oil field
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham and Jaysh al-Islam co-signed a request to IS leader.

edges.evidence_quote: On 18 February 2018, Ahrar al-Sham merged with the Nour al-Din al-Zenki Movement to form the Syrian Liberation Front.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham merged with Nour al-Din al-Zenki Movement to form Syrian Liberation Front.

edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham merged to become part of Syrian Liberation Front.

edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham and Free Syrian Army both fought against al-Assad, but no direct relationship stated.

edges.evidence_quote: It was the principal organization operating under the umbrella of the Syrian Islamic Front and was a major component of the Islamic Front.
edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham was a major component of the Islamic Front.

edges.context: A top al-Qaeda leader co-founded Ahrar al-Sham, indicating a structural link.

edges.evidence_quote: through a merger of Ahrar al-Sham's Jaysh al-Ahrar faction, Jabhat Fatah al-Sham, Nur al-Din Zenki and other militia groups
edges.context: Jaysh al-Ahrar was a faction of Ahrar al-Sham that merged into Tahrir al-Sham.

edges.evidence_quote: through a merger of Ahrar al-Sham's Jaysh al-Ahrar faction, Jabhat Fatah al-Sham, Nur al-Din Zenki and other militia groups
edges.context: Jabhat Fatah al-Sham merged with Ahrar al-Sham's faction to form Tahrir al-Sham.

edges.context: Ahrar al-Sham jointly participated in attacks with Alwiya al-Furqan.

edges.context: Ahfad al-Rasul Brigades supported protests against Ahrar al-Sham.

edges.context: Syrian Revolutionaries Front criticized the Islamic Front, of which Ahrar al-Sham was a member.

edges.evidence_quote: Jaysh al-Ahrar (Left HTS in mid-2017 due to disagreements with the leadership becoming an independent faction, later joined the National Front for Liberation with Ahrar al-Sham in August 2018)
edges.context: Jaysh al-Ahrar joined the National Front for Liberation with Ahrar al-Sham.
